title: Using advanced GitHub Copilot features
objectives: Master inline chat, slash commands, and agents

sections.title: Advanced Features Overview
sections.body:
Advanced features enable faster, more efficient development workflows. This module covers:
- **Inline Chat** — Scoped conversations using Ctrl+I (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+I (Mac)
- **Slash Commands** — Shortcuts like /tests, /explain, /suggest, /comment
- **Agents** — Specialized context for multi-step workflows
- **Implicit Prompts** — Commands that work without long explanations

sections.title: The Three-Step Workflow
sections.body:
**Step 1: Add a New Route**
Open main.py and use inline chat (Ctrl+I / Cmd+I) with prompt:
> Create a new route that exposes the cities of a country/region.

**Step 2: Create a Test**
Generate a test using the /tests command:
> /tests help me create a new test for this route that uses Spain as the country/region.

**Step 3: Write Documentation**
Use agent-based prompt in Copilot Chat:
> I want to document how to run this project so other developers can get started quickly by reading the README.md file.

This three-step pattern demonstrates complete workflow integration.
bullets: Step 1: Inline chat for route creation

sections.title: Slash Commands and Implicit Prompts
sections.body:
**Common Slash Commands:**
- `/explain` — Explains selected code
- `/suggest` — Suggests code based on context
- `/tests` — Generates unit tests
- `/comment` — Converts comments to code

**Implicit Prompts** — Use commands without repeating full context, making interactions faster and more focused when intent is clear.
bullets: Implicit prompts when intent is clear
Combine with constraints for best results

sections.title: Quality and Validation
sections.body: Advanced features increase speed but require consistent validation. Generated routes must match API contracts. Generated tests need assertion coverage. Generated documentation must be executable and complete. Never skip review despite command shortcuts.

sections.title: Advanced Workflow Practice: Inventory Update Route, Tests, and Docs
sections.body:
### Real-World Use Case
Business Context:
A warehouse team needs a new endpoint to register incoming stock and update item availability. Release cannot proceed without tests and updated developer documentation.

Developer Task:
Use advanced Copilot features to implement POST /stock-updates, generate tests, and update README for the Inventory API.

Example Input:
```json
{
  "updateId": "UPD-2026-031",
  "itemId": "ITM-9001",
  "quantityAdded": 120,
  "warehouse": "MAIN",
  "receivedAt": "2026-06-21T14:10:00Z"
}
```

Expected Output:
- Route creates stock update and increments item quantity.
- Tests cover success, missing item, duplicate updateId, and invalid quantity.
- README includes endpoint usage and run/test commands.

Why This Matters:
Accurate stock updates prevent order failures and customer complaints.

GitHub Copilot Challenge:
Use inline chat for route generation, /tests for test scaffolding, and agent mode for documentation; then refactor for idempotency.

exercise.title: Implement inventory stock update workflow with Copilot
steps: Use /tests to create success and failure test cases.
Add idempotency checks for repeated updateId.
Use agent mode to write README endpoint docs.
expectedOutcomes: Complete stock update workflow with tests and docs.
Practical use of inline chat, slash commands, and agents.
troubleshooting: If stock is updated twice, enforce idempotency checks.
If tests are flaky, isolate fixtures and reset state.
If docs are vague, request endpoint-specific examples.

quiz.question: What is ghost text in GitHub Copilot?
options: Ghost text refers to suggestions that appear in your text editor as you type.
Ghost text are options used when typing to provide suggestions.
Ghost text involves using prompts and questions within your code or documentation.
quiz.discussionPrompt: How would you apply this concept to your own projects?

quiz.question: How do you access GitHub Copilot's inline chat?
options: Click on the chat icon in the left sidebar of VS Code.
Use Ctrl+I on Windows or Cmd+I on Mac to open inline chat.
Use Alt+I on Windows or Option+I on Mac.
quiz.discussionPrompt: How would you apply this concept to your own projects?

quiz.question: What are slash commands used for?
options: Slash commands format your codebase according to best practices.
Slash commands debug code and detect security vulnerabilities.
Slash commands are shortcuts to quickly perform common development tasks.
quiz.discussionPrompt: How would you apply this concept to your own projects?

quiz.question: What are the benefits of using agents like @terminal?
options: Agents help you ask questions within a specific context for more precise answers.
Agents enforce consistent code format based on best practices.
Agents provide security features for detecting vulnerabilities.
quiz.discussionPrompt: How would you apply this concept to your own projects?

quiz.question: What are the benefits of using implicit prompts with slash commands?
options: Implicit prompts enforce consistent naming and syntax conventions.
Implicit prompts get better responses without writing longer prompts.
Implicit prompts detect security vulnerabilities in your code.
quiz.discussionPrompt: How would you apply this concept to your own projects?
